data.title: ConocoPhillips, partners submit $2B plan to redevelop Greater Ekofisk gas fields
data.description:
ConocoPhillips (COP) and its partners submitted plans Friday to the Norwegian government to spend NOK20B (~$2.1B) to restart production at three fields in the Greater Ekofisk area by the end of 2028.

The development will comprise 11 wells and four subsea templates tied back to the Ekofisk complex via a shared multiphase pipeline; Albuskjell will include two subsea templates and six wells, while Vest Ekofisk and Tommeliten Gamma will each have one subsea template with three and two associated wells, respectively.

The three fields were previously shut in before end-of-life due to decommissioning of infrastructure and limited processing capacity at Ekofisk.

ConocoPhillips (COP) estimates the Albuskjell, Vest Ekofisk, and Tommeliten Gamma fields still hold 90M-120M boe in natural gas and condensate; the company owns a 35.1% stake in the Albuskjell and Vest Ekofisk fields, and 28.3% in Tommeliten Gamma, with partners that include Vaar Energi, Orlen Upstream and state-owned Petoro.

First gas is expected in Q4 2028, subject to regulatory approval, with peak production estimated at 36K gross boe/day.

data.title: ‘The new momentum trade is rotation’ – Charles Schwab’s Liz Ann Sonders
data.description:
Liz Ann Sonders, chief investment strategist at Charles Schwab, says market rotation has emerged as the dominant trading pattern, with investors rapidly shifting focus from one opportunity to the next amid ongoing AI disruption.

“The new momentum trade is rotation,” Sonders said in an interview with CNBC, describing an environment where “short attention span money” perpetually chases the latest market narrative while selling first and researching later.

The strategist noted that AI-driven narratives are creating a volatile backdrop where micro segments of the market face sudden disruption, often leading to indiscriminate selling.

“Baby-out-with-the-bathwater kind of trading backdrop,” Sonders explained, adding that dip-buyers are becoming more selective rather than broadly lifting the entire market. Institutional investors in particular are looking for opportunities in areas that have become oversold following rapid 180-degree narrative shifts.

Despite the choppiness, Sonders believes the broadening out of market participation has staying power. She pointed to data showing that only 20% of stocks within the S&P 500 (SP500) have outperformed the index over the last year, compared to roughly 60% over the last month.

However, she cautioned that it is no longer as simple as just buying equal-weight indices (RSP) relative to cap-weight—rapid-fire sector rotations will continue.

The fundamental case for broader market participation rests on shifting earnings dynamics. While the Magnificent Seven tech giants—Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOGL), Meta Platforms (META), Tesla (TSLA), and Nvidia (NVDA)—still show stronger growth relative to the rest of the market, Sonders emphasized that “the direction of travel matters as much as the level,” noting a deceleration in their earnings growth pace alongside a pickup in expectations for more cyclical sectors.

This earnings shift also explains why non-U.S. markets are outperforming the tech-heavy American market. “Our market has been very high-tech driven, very knowledge driven, where it’s more of those traditional cyclical areas driving the economies of many overseas markets,” Sonders said.

She added that “international diversification has paid dividends this year” as a result of these fundamental differences.

Looking ahead, Sonders expects the volatile rotation pattern to persist as narratives continue to shift rapidly. While the trading environment remains challenging to navigate, she maintains that the longer-term fundamental supports for wider market participation remain intact beneath the surface turbulence.

data.title: Palo Alto Networks Q4 Preview: Deceleration fears offset upbeat earnings projection
data.description:
Palo Alto Networks (PANW) is projected to report robust second-quarter earnings, with earnings seen rising 16%, on February 17 after markets close.

The consensus EPS Estimate is $0.94, while revenue is seen jumping 14.15% to $2.58B. Over the last 3 months, EPS estimates have seen 31 upward revisions and 4 downward moves. Revenue estimates have seen 30 upward revisions and 12 downward moves.

Last quarter, the company guided for revenue between $2.57 billion and $2.59 billion and diluted non-GAAP EPS in the range of $0.93 to $0.95. The company saw next-gen ARR growth rate in the range of $6.11 billion and $6.14 billion and RPO between $15.75 billion and $15.85 billion.

Seeking Alpha analyst Bay Area Ideas said that the deceleration in the firm’s NGS ARR growth rate could be a sign that AI solutions could be losing steam.

“Furthermore, both the projected RPO and total revenue figures shown above represent slowdowns, and so there are very real indications that demand is softening and that momentum is weakening in their business overall,” Bay Area Ideas noted.

The analyst had a Sell rating on the stock due to its soft guidance, risk factors, and a premium valuation, which it believes outweighs PANW's recent operational strength.

“For the full year, the slowdown story is perhaps even more pronounced. Back in 2025, they saw next-gen security ARR growth of 32%, and so the 26.5% midpoint is really a considerable deterioration. Like for Q2, RPO and total revenue percentage increases are also expected to moderate, while margin strength may come a bit to the rescue again for the full year,” it added.

However, over the last 2 years, PANW has beaten EPS as well as revenue estimates 100% of the time, which should offer investors some optimism.

Investors are also likely to look out for remarks regarding Palo Alto’s closing of a $25B deal to acquire Israeli-based CyberArk. In Q1, the company had reiterated its 40%+ adjusted free cash flow margin target for FY '28, including both CyberArk and Chronosphere.

data.title: Restart Life to acquire Holy Crap Foods for $1M in cash
data.description:
• Restart Life Sciences (NMLSF) has signed a definitive agreement dated February 12, 2026, to acquire 100% of Holy Crap Foods.
  • The seller is Happy Belly Coffee, a subsidiary of Happy Belly Food Group (HBFGF), which owns all issued and outstanding shares of Holy Crap.
  • The $1M cash purchase price is subject to customary working capital adjustments under the agreement.
  • The definitive agreement supersedes the previously announced binding letter of intent following completion of due diligence.
  • Closing of the acquisition is expected within ten business days.
  • The acquisition includes Holy Crap’s portfolio of brands and its British Columbia-based manufacturing facility.
  • Upon closing, Holy Crap will operate as a wholly owned subsidiary of Restart Life.

data.title: Bessent continues to push Congress to pass crypto bill
data.description:
U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent once again urged lawmakers to pass the CLARITY bill and get it to President Donald Trump's desk this spring during an interview aired on Friday.

The comments come after a particularly rough patch for crypto markets and bitcoin (BTC-USD), specifically. In the past two weeks, bitcoin fell by ~19%, recently trading at ~$67.8K, down from $84.2K on Jan. 30.

"Part of the volatility is self-induced," Bessent said in an interview on CNBC, referring to the delay in getting the crypto market structure legislation passed.

This is one area where there's bipartisan support for the legislation, he noted, but some crypto firms have been lobbying against it. Coinbase (COIN), in particular, opposed a Senate draft of the legislation last month, citing its approach to stablecoin rewards and certain safeguards related to decentralized finance.

"They'd rather have no legislation than this legislation," he said. "That doesn't seem to have been good for the overall crypto communities."

Bessent said it is critical that CLARITY be enacted before the November midterm elections. If the Democrats take control of the House, "then the prospects of getting a deal done will just fall apart," he said.

He said that under the Biden administration, "it was almost an extinction event" for crypto.

"I think it's important to get this CLARITY bill done as soon as possible and on the president's desk this spring," Bessent concluded.

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) has recovered some from its recent low of $60.2K on Feb. 6, rising to $68.3K at ~10:11 AM ET.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: AtlasClear outlines scaling strategy with 84% revenue growth and targets correspondent clearing expansion in 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: AtlasClear Holdings, Inc. (ATCH) Q2 2026

Management View

  • John Schaible, Executive Chairman, highlighted that "the December quarter marks a clear inflection point for the company." He reported "revenue grew 84% year-over-year," net income of $6.8 million, positive stockholders' equity of $21.7 million—"an accomplishment that we need to highlight"—and ending the quarter with $46.2 million in cash and restricted cash. Schaible emphasized that "the current market environment is becoming increasingly constructive for our model" as smaller and midsized institutions seek efficient, flexible infrastructure.
  • President Craig Ridenhour stated the quarter is "about momentum becoming visible and a growing sense of optimism about where the platform is headed." He described Wilson-Davis as continuing to perform as the "core clearing engine of the platform," with strength across commissions, stock locate services, and clearing-related activity. He noted "excess net capital we're carrying provides the capacity to onboard additional relationships and expand services without stretching the balance sheet."
  • Sandip Patel, CFO, said, "For the fourth quarter ended December 31, 2025, AtlasClear reported revenue of $5.1 million, representing an 84% increase year-over-year." He explained commissions were "the largest contributor at just over $3 million for the quarter," with other revenues also contributing. Patel added, "the company reported net income of $6.8 million, which includes noncash fair value adjustments" and ended with $46.2 million in cash and restricted cash. He described the company as having a "materially stronger underlying operating and financial profile than a year ago."

Outlook

  • President Ridenhour stated that priorities include "doubling down on what works" by building around Wilson-Davis, "scaling responsibly and with conviction" by onboarding the right clients, and "advancing the full AtlasClear vision," including progress toward the proposed acquisition of Commercial Bancorp of Wyoming. He said, "We're prioritizing opportunities that are accretive and fit our infrastructure, regulatory framework and capital profile, not growth for growth's sake."
  • Schaible described the path forward as "focused and deliberate, enhancing the core clearing business with smarter technology, improving connectivity across workflows and ensuring the platform remains adaptable as markets, client needs in areas like fintech and regulated digital assets continue to evolve."

Financial Results

  • AtlasClear reported revenue of $5.1 million for the quarter, with commissions contributing just over $3 million. Net income for the quarter was $6.8 million, which includes noncash fair value adjustments. Patel stated that "expenses increased in line with revenue growth, primarily due to variable compensation, clearing and data processing costs and stock-based compensation associated with new executive employment agreements." Stockholders' equity increased to $21.7 million, and total assets rose to $77.6 million.
  • Liquidity was strengthened with $46.2 million in cash and restricted cash at quarter-end. Wilson-Davis net capital totaled $14.7 million, supporting higher levels of client activity and providing capacity to onboard new correspondent relationships.

Q&A

  • Jeff Ramson, ProActive Capital Group, LLC, asked about the fully diluted share count and whether the company is past the heavy conversion phase. Patel responded, "The current outstanding share count is approximately 150 million shares... on a fully diluted basis, at the current exercise price of $0.75 a share, this would be approximately an additional 43 million shares on the warrants granted in the units and a little over 14 million shares on the convertible note if converted."
  • Ramson asked about corporate liquidity versus regulatory capital, and Schaible explained, "the threshold that we really always have to stay above is $10.5 million... we can't maintain our correspondent license if we go below it. Fortunately, we've been able to significantly increase our regulatory capital as well."
  • On the revenue level for consistent operating leverage at Wilson-Davis, Schaible said, "our operating costs last year were in the neighborhood of $14 million... Once we cover that, then we can scale tremendously upon our platform."
  • Ramson inquired about the expected ramp timeline for new brokerage firms. Ridenhour stated, "we're optimistic that [Dawson James will] be trading here momentarily... every subsequent correspondent clearing client for us should come on much more quickly and with greater ease."
  • Asked for an update on the Commercial Bancorp acquisition, Ridenhour said, "we are preparing to file our application with the Fed... we're optimistic that this will move along more quickly and we'll ultimately get the approval." Schaible added, "the bank will be immediately accretive to us. In '25, the bank did about $1.9 million in revenue with $500,000 in net income."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on capital structure, dilution, liquidity, scalability, and acquisition timelines, with a generally inquisitive but neutral tone, pressing for details on share count and operational thresholds.
  • Management's tone was confident and slightly optimistic, emphasizing phrases such as "we're optimistic," "we continue to see a healthy pipeline," and "we think that's going to happen through 2026." Responses were transparent, and management addressed potential concerns directly, with no notable defensiveness or hesitation.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment has shifted from caution regarding liquidity and listing compliance to more focus on growth execution and the pace of operational leverage. Management's tone in both quarters remained confident but is now more assertive about execution and near-term milestones.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue increased from $4.25 million in the previous quarter to $5.1 million in the current quarter, while net income improved from a net loss of $440,000 to net income of $6.8 million.
  • Stockholders' equity moved from $6.86 million to $21.7 million. Liquidity increased from $32.2 million in cash and restricted cash to $46.2 million. Wilson-Davis net capital also rose.
  • The previous quarter focused on stabilization, balance sheet repair, and laying groundwork for growth; the current quarter emphasizes scaling the platform, onboarding new clients, and advancing acquisitions.
  • Analysts' questions shifted from financing structure and exchange compliance to growth scalability, operational leverage, and acquisition integration.
  • Management confidence has increased, with more discussion of scaling and operational momentum.

Risks and Concerns

  • Investors remain concerned about dilution and outstanding convertibles; Patel stated "we fully expect any additional dilution to be accretive."
  • Liquidity versus regulatory capital was addressed by Schaible, who clarified the minimum thresholds required for licensing and operational flexibility.
  • The pace of onboarding new brokerage clients and the timing of revenue impact was discussed, with management indicating optimism but acknowledging internal system upgrades were required.
  • Regulatory approval timing for the Commercial Bancorp acquisition remains a variable, but management is "optimistic" about the process.

Final Takeaway

AtlasClear management reported a decisive inflection in its financial and operational profile, supported by substantial revenue and equity growth, robust liquidity, and a clear focus on scaling its correspondent clearing business through the Wilson-Davis platform. Management underscored operational leverage and growth from new client onboarding, while emphasizing disciplined expansion and the strategic value of the proposed Commercial Bancorp acquisition. The company concluded that its strengthened foundation, improved balance sheet, and expanding opportunities position it well for durable growth and long-term shareholder value.

data.title: Rithm Property Trust outlines $1.60–$1.70 EPS target through planned recap and multifamily loan growth
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Rithm Property Trust Inc. (RPT) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Michael Nierenberg, President and CEO, highlighted a light investment quarter aside from a small Paramount transaction investment, reiterating, "the balance sheet, cash, the company remains in great shape."
  • Nierenberg announced a 6:1 reverse share split, aiming to boost share price visibility, stating, "We feel like it's going to hopefully attract more interest in the stock with a higher -- obviously, a higher share price."
  • The CEO emphasized the company's transformation since June 2024, describing a focus on recapitalization and future earnings and dividend growth. "We have a clear path...to take the company from flat earnings to a future state where the company is earning something between $1.60 and $1.70 per share and trades, give or take, about a 9% dividend yield with a book value of approximately $20."
  • Nierenberg detailed a growth plan centered on acquiring multifamily loans from the Genesis operating business and other commercial real estate investments, noting, "there will be no J-curve as we think about earnings growth and where we're going with the vehicle."
  • Expanding in commercial real estate, he referenced the Paramount acquisition and the potential to acquire Fannie or Freddie licenses for multifamily lending.
  • The CEO underscored a diversified earnings model, referencing strong performance at the parent level and the intent to replicate that at RPT: "We're very good at...creating diversified earnings streams."

Outlook

  • Nierenberg outlined the path to earnings of $1.60–$1.70 per share, contingent on a recapitalization and new capital formation.
  • The plan includes deploying identified multifamily loans and other commercial assets, with the expectation of immediate earnings impact upon capital deployment.
  • The CEO indicated patience in executing the recap, stating, "we will be patient, we hope to accomplish this when the markets stabilize."
  • A continued focus on opportunistic investments and expanding multifamily loan production, potentially reaching $6 billion–$7 billion this year.

Financial Results

  • Nierenberg reported fourth quarter GAAP earnings of $2.5 million, with "EAD...kind of $500,000 to the negative, which leads to a per diluted share of $0.06 negative."
  • Book value was cited as about $300 million, or $31 per diluted share.
  • The common stock dividend yield is 8.7%, with approximately $100 million in cash and liquidity.
  • The company's trading price is around 50% of book value.

Q&A

  • Craig Kucera, Lucid Capital Markets: Asked if RPT will receive a share of Paramount NOI and the earnings impact. Michael Nierenberg responded, "It's a pro rata share of what Rithm did on the balance sheet. So when you look at it, RPT has $50 million of the Paramount deal in -- on its balance sheet, and it will be pro rata versus Rithm."
  • Kucera: Inquired about Genesis-originated loan deployment and timing for recap. Nierenberg highlighted $6–$7 billion expected production at Genesis and clarified, "if we're successful around a capital raise for RPT, there'll be loans that we've identified. So...there is no J-curve."
  • Henry Coffey, Wedbush: Asked about timing and capital sources for recap. Nierenberg replied, "there's third-party capital that wants to be part of the vehicle...we're not going to leave this vehicle outstanding trading as where it does forever."
  • Coffey: Questioned loan mix for RPT. Nierenberg said, "what we've identified as a pool of assets...something around $1 billion of assets that would go right into the vehicle...you'd see an immediate pop in earnings."
  • Jason Stewart, Compass Point: Asked about sourcing loans from Genesis versus third parties. Nierenberg stated, "we are going to source from third parties wherever we can as long as we're comfortable with the credit."
  • Stewart: Inquired about the strategic goal of Fannie/Freddie licensing. Nierenberg confirmed, "end-to-end is something that we're trying to do for sure."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst questions reflected cautious optimism, centering on timing, recapitalization, and capital sources, without overt skepticism or negativity.
  • Management maintained a confident and forward-looking tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, repeatedly referencing a "clear path" to earnings growth and emphasizing patience and strategic discipline.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, there was an increase in specificity and confidence, with management now outlining an explicit earnings target and concrete steps for capital deployment.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter introduced a specific earnings target ($1.60–$1.70 per share) and a more detailed recapitalization plan involving multifamily loan acquisition and growth in Genesis loan origination.
  • In the previous quarter, management discussed options for recapitalization or even liquidation without specifying targets. This quarter, the focus shifted to patient execution of a recap and growing the company through identified asset infusions.
  • Analyst focus shifted from broad strategic alternatives in Q3 to more granular questions on capital sources, asset deployment, and timing in Q4, reflecting increased management clarity.
  • The management tone evolved from discussing stabilization and options to expressing optimism about growth and an actionable plan.

Risks and Concerns

  • Nierenberg acknowledged that "many REITs, BDCs and other capital vehicles are not trading well," reinforcing the sector's dislocation and the need for patience in recapitalization.
  • The plan's success is dependent on market stabilization and the ability to raise new capital without excessive dilution.
  • Management indicated vigilance in credit quality when sourcing third-party loans, ensuring no "missteps around the credit side."
  • While the company holds significant cash and a clean balance sheet, execution risk remains tied to timing of recap and market receptivity.

Final Takeaway

Rithm Property Trust's management articulated a focused strategy to move from flat earnings to a target of $1.60–$1.70 per share, contingent on a successful recap and deployment of multifamily and commercial real estate loans through Genesis and other channels. The company maintains a strong liquidity position and clean balance sheet, positioning itself to capitalize on sector dislocation with a disciplined and patient approach to capital formation. The explicit earnings goal and detailed operational plan mark a shift from last quarter’s exploratory tone to a more confident and actionable growth strategy.
